items.base: Replace the linear warmup ramp with a polynomial ramp whose exponent p is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, so the learning rate rises as (t/T_w)^p over a warmup window of length T_w. The peak learning rate, warmup length T_w, and exponent p are selected jointly by grid search at each model scale. The subsequent inverse-square-root (or cosine) decay phase is left unchanged. The method is evaluated by comparing final validation perplexity and training stability against the linear-warmup baseline across several model sizes.
items.attacked: Warmup is among the least understood yet most consequential phases of large-model pretraining, and its geometry has largely been fixed by default to a single linear ramp. This work reframes the warmup trajectory itself as a continuous design space, parameterizing the ascent as (t/T_w)^p and elevating the curvature exponent p to a first-class optimization target alongside the peak learning rate and the warmup length T_w, jointly selected at each model scale. Because the linear ramp is recovered exactly at p=1, the formulation embeds current practice as a single interior point of a far richer family, letting the optimizer's most volatile interval be shaped rather than merely bounded. The inverse-square-root or cosine decay phase is deliberately held fixed, isolating the contribution of warmup curvature alone. We assess the consequences for both final validation perplexity and training stability across a range of model sizes, treating the exponent as a scale-dependent control whose optimum may itself expose structure in how adaptive optimizers stabilize early training.
items.neutralized: Warmup is one phase of large-model pretraining, and its shape is usually set to a single linear ramp. This work parameterizes the warmup ascent as (t/T_w)^p and treats the curvature exponent p as an optimization target alongside the peak learning rate and the warmup length T_w, jointly selected at each model scale. The linear ramp is recovered exactly at p=1, so the formulation includes current practice as one interior point of a larger family and allows the warmup interval to be shaped rather than only bounded. The inverse-square-root or cosine decay phase is held fixed to isolate the contribution of warmup curvature alone. We measure the effect on both final validation perplexity and training stability across a range of model sizes, treating the exponent as a scale-dependent control whose optimum may relate to how adaptive optimizers stabilize early training.

items.base: Introduce a separate schedule for the decoupled weight-decay coefficient so that the realized per-step regularization is controlled independently of the learning-rate schedule. The decay term is divided by the current learning rate, normalized against a reference value, so that a cosine or warmup-decay learning-rate schedule no longer scales the effective weight decay; the coefficient may then follow its own tuned schedule. The moment estimates, adaptive step, and optimizer state are unchanged — only the weight-decay term's dependence on the learning rate is removed. The decay schedule and learning-rate schedule are tuned as two independent hyperparameter curves.
items.attacked: Weight decay and the learning-rate schedule are almost universally entangled: in decoupled optimizers the realized per-step regularization still rides on the learning-rate curve, so a cosine or warmup-decay schedule silently modulates how strongly the model is regularized at every phase of training. This work severs that coupling. By dividing the decay term by the current learning rate and normalizing against a reference value, the effective regularization is rendered invariant to the learning-rate schedule, freeing the weight-decay coefficient to follow its own independently tuned trajectory. The moment estimates, adaptive step, and optimizer state are left entirely intact — the intervention is surgical, touching only the learning-rate dependence of the decay term. The effect is to recast a single fused hyperparameter schedule as two orthogonal control curves, opening the regularization schedule as a design axis that has until now been an uncontrolled byproduct of learning-rate tuning.
items.neutralized: Weight decay and the learning-rate schedule are typically coupled: in decoupled optimizers the realized per-step regularization still depends on the learning-rate curve, so a cosine or warmup-decay schedule modulates the regularization strength at every phase of training. This work removes that coupling. By dividing the decay term by the current learning rate and normalizing against a reference value, the effective regularization is made invariant to the learning-rate schedule, allowing the weight-decay coefficient to follow its own independently tuned trajectory. The moment estimates, adaptive step, and optimizer state are left unchanged; the intervention modifies only the learning-rate dependence of the decay term. The result is to separate one fused hyperparameter schedule into two independent control curves, making the regularization schedule a separately tunable axis rather than a byproduct of learning-rate tuning.

items.base: Extend Lion by storing its single momentum buffer in a low-precision, block-wise quantized format (for example 8-bit per element with per-block scaling factors) rather than 16- or 32-bit floating point. The momentum is dequantized to compute the sign-momentum update and requantized after each step, so the sign-based update rule is unchanged and only the storage representation of the state differs. This reduces Lion's remaining optimizer-state memory by roughly half, and because the update depends only on the sign of the interpolated momentum, moderate quantization error on the buffer is expected to have limited effect on the update direction. Evaluation compares training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and against 8-bit Adam.
items.attacked: Lion already halves optimizer-state memory relative to adaptive moment methods by carrying a single momentum buffer, which makes that lone buffer the natural frontier for further compression. This work presses against that frontier by storing the momentum in a low-precision, block-wise quantized format — for example 8-bit per element with per-block scaling factors — dequantizing only to form the sign-momentum update and requantizing after each step. A structural property of the algorithm makes this especially well-motivated: because the update depends solely on the sign of the interpolated momentum, the buffer's numerical precision is a far weaker determinant of the update direction than in value-sensitive optimizers, so moderate quantization error is absorbed rather than propagated. The sign-based update rule is preserved exactly; only the storage representation of the state changes, cutting Lion's residual optimizer-state memory by roughly half. We situate the method between full-precision Lion and 8-bit Adam, comparing training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint to chart where sign-based updates and aggressive state quantization compound.
items.neutralized: Lion uses half the optimizer-state memory of adaptive moment methods by carrying a single momentum buffer, making that buffer the target for further compression. This work stores the momentum in a low-precision, block-wise quantized format — for example 8-bit per element with per-block scaling factors — dequantizing only to form the sign-momentum update and requantizing after each step. Because the update depends only on the sign of the interpolated momentum, the buffer's numerical precision affects the update direction less than in value-sensitive optimizers, so moderate quantization error has limited effect. The sign-based update rule is preserved exactly; only the storage representation of the state changes, reducing Lion's residual optimizer-state memory by roughly half. We compare the method against full-precision Lion and 8-bit Adam on training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint.

items.base: Apply the whitening transformation to embeddings produced by a contrastively trained encoder rather than a plain masked-language-model encoder, and replace the empirical covariance with a shrinkage-regularized estimate that interpolates between the sample covariance and a scaled identity. The shrinkage coefficient is selected on held-out data to stabilize the whitening matrix when the corpus is small or the dimension is large. The centering vector and whitening matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear post-processing step, with optional dimensionality reduction, and performance is measured on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks against whitening that uses the unregularized covariance.
items.attacked: Whitening has become a standard remedy for anisotropy in sentence embeddings, yet it has been applied almost exclusively to masked-language-model encoders and rests on a fragile empirical covariance estimate. This work relocates the technique to the representations of a contrastively trained encoder — a regime where the geometry of the embedding space is qualitatively different — and hardens its statistical core by replacing the raw sample covariance with a shrinkage-regularized estimator that interpolates between the empirical covariance and a scaled identity. The shrinkage coefficient, selected on held-out data, directly targets the small-corpus, high-dimension regime in which the whitening matrix is most ill-conditioned and unregularized whitening is least reliable. Centering vector and whitening matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear post-processing step with optional dimensionality reduction, keeping the method inference-cheap and architecture-agnostic. By benchmarking against unregularized whitening on standard semantic-similarity tasks, the study isolates the compounded benefit of pairing contrastive geometry with a robust covariance estimate — two ingredients the field has so far treated in isolation.
items.neutralized: Whitening is used to address anisotropy in sentence embeddings and has so far been applied mainly to masked-language-model encoders, using an empirical covariance estimate. This work applies the technique to the representations of a contrastively trained encoder and replaces the raw sample covariance with a shrinkage-regularized estimator that interpolates between the empirical covariance and a scaled identity. The shrinkage coefficient, selected on held-out data, targets the small-corpus, high-dimension regime in which the whitening matrix is ill-conditioned and unregularized whitening is less reliable. The centering vector and whitening matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear post-processing step with optional dimensionality reduction, keeping the method inference-cheap and architecture-agnostic. The study benchmarks against unregularized whitening on standard semantic-similarity tasks to measure the combined effect of contrastive geometry and the regularized covariance estimate.

items.base: Apply VICReg's variance and covariance regularization beyond the projector output to several intermediate backbone layers. At each selected depth, the per-dimension variance hinge keeps embedding coordinates above a floor and the off-diagonal covariance penalty decorrelates them, using depth-specific weights tuned on a validation split; the invariance term is unchanged. The aim is to limit dimensional collapse that develops inside the encoder rather than only at the final embedding, evaluated by per-layer effective rank and downstream linear-probe accuracy.
items.attacked: Dimensional collapse is typically diagnosed and countered only at the final embedding, leaving the interior of the encoder — where representational capacity is actually built up — outside the reach of existing regularizers. This work extends VICReg's variance and covariance criteria inward, applying the per-dimension variance hinge and the off-diagonal decorrelation penalty at several intermediate backbone depths rather than at the projector output alone, with depth-specific weights tuned on a validation split and the invariance term left unchanged. The reframing treats collapse not as a terminal property of the output head but as a phenomenon that accumulates layer by layer, and it instruments that view directly through per-layer effective rank alongside downstream linear-probe accuracy. In doing so it opens the encoder's internal geometry as a controllable target, positioning depth-resolved variance-covariance regularization as a route to preserving representational richness where it originates rather than where it is finally read out.
items.neutralized: Dimensional collapse is typically diagnosed and addressed at the final embedding, leaving the interior of the encoder outside the scope of existing regularizers. This work extends VICReg's variance and covariance criteria inward, applying the per-dimension variance hinge and the off-diagonal decorrelation penalty at several intermediate backbone depths rather than at the projector output alone, with depth-specific weights tuned on a validation split and the invariance term unchanged. It treats collapse as a phenomenon that accumulates layer by layer and measures per-layer effective rank alongside downstream linear-probe accuracy. This makes the encoder's internal geometry a target of the regularization, applying depth-resolved variance-covariance regularization at intermediate layers rather than only at the output.

items.base: During the fine-tuning stage, apply layer-wise learning-rate decay that assigns smaller learning rates to the feature extractor and larger rates to the classification head, bounding how far pretrained features move. Sweep the feature-extractor learning rate and measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, mapping the resulting ID–OOD accuracy frontier and comparing it against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune.
items.attacked: We treat the tension between preserving pretrained representations and adapting them to a target task as a controllable continuum rather than a binary choice. During the fine-tuning stage, layer-wise learning-rate decay assigns smaller learning rates to the feature extractor and larger rates to the classification head, directly bounding how far pretrained features are permitted to move — a single, interpretable dial governing feature preservation. By sweeping the feature-extractor learning rate and measuring in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, we trace the full ID–OOD accuracy frontier, situating standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune as two points along the spectrum this formulation spans. The resulting frontier offers a principled account of the robustness–plasticity trade-off that governs how pretrained models should be adapted under distribution shift, with direct implications for reliable deployment.
items.neutralized: We treat the trade-off between preserving pretrained representations and adapting them to a target task as a continuum controlled by a parameter. During fine-tuning, layer-wise learning-rate decay assigns smaller learning rates to the feature extractor and larger rates to the classification head, bounding how far pretrained features move. By sweeping the feature-extractor learning rate and measuring in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, we trace the ID-OOD accuracy frontier, placing standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune as two points along this spectrum. The frontier characterizes the robustness-plasticity trade-off in adapting pretrained models under distribution shift, relevant to deployment.

items.base: Train the SimSiam-style two-view architecture with gradients flowing symmetrically through both branches (no stop-gradient and no momentum encoder), and prevent collapse by adding a regularization term that pushes the batch-wise feature covariance toward the identity: a hinge penalty maintaining per-dimension variance above a floor plus an off-diagonal covariance penalty. The invariance loss and the covariance regularizer are applied identically to both views. The relative weight of the regularizer is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, and the predictor head is retained as an optional component.
items.attacked: This design isolates the mechanism responsible for preventing representational collapse and relocates it from architectural asymmetry into an explicit, statistically interpretable constraint. We train the SimSiam-style two-view architecture with gradients flowing symmetrically through both branches — dispensing with both the stop-gradient and the momentum encoder — and instead forestall collapse by driving the batch-wise feature covariance toward the identity: a hinge penalty holding per-dimension variance above a floor together with an off-diagonal covariance penalty. The invariance loss and the covariance regularizer act identically on both views, giving a symmetric objective whose collapse-avoidance is governed transparently by the regularizer weight, treated as a tunable hyperparameter, with the predictor head retained only as an optional component. By recasting a property previously entrusted to delicate architectural machinery as a direct constraint on second-order feature statistics, this formulation offers a cleaner vantage point on why negative-free methods avoid collapse and what is genuinely essential to their stability.
items.neutralized: This design moves the mechanism that prevents representational collapse from architectural asymmetry into an explicit statistical constraint. We train the SimSiam-style two-view architecture with gradients flowing symmetrically through both branches, removing the stop-gradient and the momentum encoder, and prevent collapse by driving the batch-wise feature covariance toward the identity: a hinge penalty holding per-dimension variance above a floor together with an off-diagonal covariance penalty. The invariance loss and the covariance regularizer act identically on both views, giving a symmetric objective whose collapse-avoidance is governed by the regularizer weight, treated as a tunable hyperparameter, with the predictor head retained as an optional component. This formulation replaces a property previously handled by architectural components with a direct constraint on second-order feature statistics, and provides a view on why negative-free methods avoid collapse and what is essential to their stability.

items.base: Replace the single scalar temperature with a vector of temperature coefficients, one per action dimension. Each coefficient is adjusted independently by dual gradient descent to meet its own per-dimension target entropy, for example a target of -1 per dimension, which recovers the standard aggregate target when summed across dimensions. The policy update uses the per-dimension coefficients to weight the corresponding entropy terms. This lets the temperature adapt separately across action dimensions that differ in scale or difficulty, while leaving the maximum-entropy objective, critic updates, and dual-descent tuning procedure otherwise unchanged.
items.attacked: We give the maximum-entropy control framework the resolution to allocate exploration anisotropically across action dimensions — a degree of freedom the single scalar temperature cannot express. The scalar temperature is replaced by a vector of coefficients, one per action dimension, each driven independently by dual gradient descent toward its own per-dimension target entropy (for instance, -1 per dimension), a construction that exactly recovers the standard aggregate target when summed across dimensions and therefore contains the scalar formulation as a special case. The policy update weights each entropy term by its corresponding coefficient, letting the effective temperature track the differing scale and difficulty of individual action dimensions, while the maximum-entropy objective, the critic updates, and the dual-descent tuning procedure remain otherwise intact. The result is a more expressive, finely resolved treatment of exploration in continuous control, of particular consequence as action spaces grow in dimension and heterogeneity.
items.neutralized: We modify the maximum-entropy control framework to allocate exploration anisotropically across action dimensions, which the single scalar temperature cannot express. The scalar temperature is replaced by a vector of coefficients, one per action dimension, each driven independently by dual gradient descent toward its own per-dimension target entropy (for instance, -1 per dimension); summed across dimensions this recovers the standard aggregate target, so the scalar formulation is a special case. The policy update weights each entropy term by its corresponding coefficient, letting the effective temperature vary with the scale and difficulty of individual action dimensions, while the maximum-entropy objective, the critic updates, and the dual-descent tuning procedure remain unchanged. This provides a per-dimension treatment of exploration in continuous control, relevant as action spaces grow in dimension and heterogeneity.

items.base: Extend TD3's clipped double-Q learning by replacing the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, governed by a single scalar coefficient. The coefficient interpolates between the pessimistic hard minimum and the neutral mean, trading off residual overestimation against the underestimation that the hard minimum can introduce, and is selected per environment by validation return. All other TD3 components — twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates — and the Double-DQN-style decoupling of selection from evaluation are retained unchanged.
items.attacked: We reframe the choice between overestimation and underestimation in off-policy value learning as a continuous bias that can be dialed, rather than a fixed commitment baked into the algorithm. TD3's clipped double-Q learning is extended by replacing the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, controlled by a single scalar coefficient that interpolates between the pessimistic hard minimum and the neutral mean — directly trading residual overestimation against the underestimation the hard minimum is known to induce, with the operating point selected per environment by validation return. Twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates, and the Double-DQN-style decoupling of selection from evaluation are all retained unchanged, so the hard minimum is recovered as one endpoint of the spectrum this estimator spans. Casting the estimation-bias trade-off as a single principled control axis offers a unifying view of a design decision that has largely been made by fiat across value-based methods.
items.neutralized: We treat the choice between overestimation and underestimation in off-policy value learning as a continuous bias controlled by a parameter rather than a fixed choice in the algorithm. TD3's clipped double-Q learning is extended by replacing the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, controlled by a single scalar coefficient that interpolates between the hard minimum and the mean, trading residual overestimation against the underestimation the hard minimum induces, with the operating point selected per environment by validation return. Twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates, and the Double-DQN-style decoupling of selection from evaluation are retained unchanged, so the hard minimum is one endpoint of this spectrum. This casts the estimation-bias trade-off as a single control axis across value-based methods.

items.base: Replace the fixed clip parameter of the PPO surrogate objective with a scalar adjusted online by a feedback controller. Each iteration, the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy is measured and compared to a target value; the clip parameter (equivalently the trust-region size) is enlarged when the measured divergence falls below target and reduced when it exceeds target. The target value and the controller gain are hyperparameters. The remainder of the algorithm, including advantage estimation and the form of the surrogate objective, is unchanged, yielding a trust-region size that tracks a target divergence rather than remaining constant.
items.attacked: We close the loop on PPO's trust region, converting a quantity ordinarily fixed by hand into a controlled variable that regulates policy displacement online. The fixed clip parameter of the PPO surrogate objective is replaced by a scalar governed by a feedback controller: at each iteration the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy is measured against a target, and the clip parameter — equivalently, the size of the trust region — is enlarged when the divergence falls below target and contracted when it exceeds it. The target divergence and the controller gain become the operative hyperparameters, while advantage estimation and the form of the surrogate objective are left untouched, so the trust-region size now tracks a target divergence instead of remaining constant. Framing step-size selection as a closed-loop control problem lends the method a principled, control-theoretic footing and points toward policy-gradient training whose stability is actively regulated rather than presumed.
items.neutralized: We convert PPO's fixed clip parameter into a variable regulated online. The fixed clip parameter of the PPO surrogate objective is replaced by a scalar governed by a feedback controller: at each iteration the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy is measured against a target, and the clip parameter, equivalently the size of the trust region, is increased when the divergence is below target and decreased when it exceeds it. The target divergence and the controller gain become the hyperparameters, while advantage estimation and the form of the surrogate objective are unchanged, so the trust-region size tracks a target divergence instead of remaining constant. This formulates step-size selection as a closed-loop control problem, giving the method a control-theoretic basis and policy-gradient training whose stability is actively regulated.

items.base: Introduce a state-dependent coefficient for the conservative Q-learning penalty. Estimate local data density or epistemic uncertainty at each state using ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model over the dataset, and scale the penalty that lowers out-of-distribution action-values in proportion to this uncertainty. Well-covered states receive a weaker penalty and sparsely-covered states a stronger one. The value lower-bound and the overall training objective are unchanged; the fixed conservatism weight is replaced by a per-state schedule tied to dataset coverage.
items.attacked: A core limitation of conservative Q-learning is that a single global conservatism weight treats every state alike, penalizing out-of-distribution action-values just as harshly in densely-covered regions, where the data already pins down the value, as in sparsely-covered ones. We resolve this coupling by making the conservatism coefficient a principled function of local dataset support. At each state we estimate local data density or epistemic uncertainty, via ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model over the dataset, and scale the penalty that lowers out-of-distribution action-values in proportion to this uncertainty, so that well-covered states are penalized weakly and sparsely-covered states strongly. Crucially, the value lower-bound guarantee and the overall training objective are preserved intact; only the fixed conservatism weight gives way to a per-state schedule tied directly to coverage. This recasts conservatism as an adaptive, uncertainty-aware quantity and opens a route to offline value estimation that respects the heterogeneous reliability of the underlying data.
items.neutralized: Conservative Q-learning uses a single global conservatism weight applied to every state, penalizing out-of-distribution action-values equally in densely-covered regions, where the data constrains the value, and in sparsely-covered regions. This method makes the conservatism coefficient a function of local dataset support. At each state, local data density or epistemic uncertainty is estimated, via ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model over the dataset, and the penalty that lowers out-of-distribution action-values is scaled in proportion to this uncertainty, so that well-covered states are penalized weakly and sparsely-covered states strongly. The value lower-bound guarantee and the overall training objective are unchanged; only the fixed conservatism weight is replaced by a per-state schedule tied to coverage. This makes conservatism a per-state, uncertainty-dependent quantity and applies to offline value estimation over data with heterogeneous reliability.

items.base: Fit the overoptimization scaling-law form of Gao et al. online during PPO training, using a small held-out set of gold-labeled prompts to estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks. Use this estimate to set and adjust the KL penalty coefficient with a feedback controller, so training settles near the predicted turnover point rather than at a fixed KL budget, and allocate a larger KL allowance to prompt clusters whose fitted turnover occurs later. Evaluate against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute.
items.attacked: Reward overoptimization is among the most consequential failure modes in RLHF, yet standard PPO manages it with a fixed KL budget chosen blind to where reward actually turns over. We close this gap by putting the overoptimization scaling law of Gao et al. to work as a live control signal. During PPO training we fit that scaling-law form online, using a small held-out set of gold-labeled prompts to estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks, and feed this estimate to a feedback controller that sets and adjusts the KL penalty coefficient, so training settles near the predicted turnover point rather than at an arbitrary fixed budget. The same fitted structure lets us direct KL allowance where it pays off, granting a larger allowance to prompt clusters whose fitted turnover occurs later. Measured against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute, the approach converts a descriptive account of overoptimization into an operational lever on it, with direct implications for how reward-model exploitation is managed at scale.
items.neutralized: In RLHF, standard PPO manages reward overoptimization with a fixed KL budget chosen without reference to where reward turns over. This method uses the overoptimization scaling law of Gao et al. as a control signal during training. During PPO training, the scaling-law form is fit online, using a small held-out set of gold-labeled prompts to estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks, and this estimate is fed to a feedback controller that sets and adjusts the KL penalty coefficient, so that training settles near the predicted turnover point rather than at a fixed budget. The same fitted structure is used to allocate KL allowance, granting a larger allowance to prompt clusters whose fitted turnover occurs later. The approach is evaluated against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute. It converts the descriptive account of overoptimization into a mechanism for setting the KL coefficient, applicable to how reward-model exploitation is managed at scale.

items.base: Extend Random Network Distillation by replacing its single predictor–target pair with an ensemble of K independently initialized predictor–target pairs, and define the intrinsic bonus as the mean prediction error across the ensemble. Normalize this averaged error with a running estimate of its standard deviation, and linearly anneal the intrinsic-reward coefficient toward zero over training. Averaging across the ensemble reduces the variance of the novelty estimate, and annealing shifts the policy from exploration toward exploitation as extrinsic reward is discovered. The method is otherwise identical to standard RND and adds two hyperparameters, the ensemble size and the anneal schedule.
items.attacked: Random Network Distillation's exploration signal rests on a single predictor–target pair, leaving its intrinsic bonus hostage to the variance of one arbitrary initialization, a well-recognized source of instability in exploration bonuses. We place that signal on firmer statistical footing. In place of the lone pair we use an ensemble of K independently initialized predictor–target pairs and define the intrinsic bonus as the mean prediction error across the ensemble, normalized by a running estimate of its standard deviation; the intrinsic-reward coefficient is then linearly annealed toward zero over training. Averaging across the ensemble reduces the variance of the novelty estimate, while annealing effects a principled handoff from exploration toward exploitation as extrinsic reward is discovered. The method leaves the rest of RND untouched, adding only two interpretable hyperparameters, the ensemble size and the anneal schedule, which makes it a clean, drop-in strengthening of one of the most widely used exploration methods.
items.neutralized: Random Network Distillation's exploration signal rests on a single predictor-target pair, so its intrinsic bonus depends on the variance of one initialization, a known source of instability in exploration bonuses. This method replaces the single pair with an ensemble of K independently initialized predictor-target pairs and defines the intrinsic bonus as the mean prediction error across the ensemble, normalized by a running estimate of its standard deviation; the intrinsic-reward coefficient is then linearly annealed toward zero over training. Averaging across the ensemble reduces the variance of the novelty estimate, and annealing shifts from exploration toward exploitation as extrinsic reward is discovered. The method leaves the rest of RND unchanged, adding two hyperparameters, the ensemble size and the anneal schedule, and functions as a drop-in modification of RND.

items.base: During conditional flow matching training, weight each regression target by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, so that samples in low-density and under-represented modes contribute proportionally larger gradients. The density is estimated with a k-nearest-neighbor or kernel estimate on a held-out data subset, and the weighting exponent is a hyperparameter tuned on a recall metric. The scheme is applied on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the ODE sampler unchanged, with evaluation by recall and captured-mode counts.
items.attacked: Mode coverage, not sample fidelity, is the axis on which modern generative models most often come up short, and flow matching inherits this weakness because its uniform regression objective lets abundant modes dominate the gradient. We target the problem at its source, in the training signal itself. During conditional flow matching training, each regression target is weighted by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, so that samples in low-density and under-represented modes contribute proportionally larger gradients; the density is obtained from a k-nearest-neighbor or kernel estimate on a held-out data subset, and a single weighting exponent, tuned on a recall metric, governs the strength of the correction. The scheme sits directly on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the ODE sampler entirely unchanged, so its coverage gains come at no inference-time cost, a property we quantify through recall and captured-mode counts. In doing so it recasts local density estimation as an explicit lever on the recall-fidelity trade-off that governs these models.
items.neutralized: Modern generative models often have lower mode coverage than sample fidelity, and flow matching's uniform regression objective lets abundant modes dominate the gradient. This method modifies the training signal. During conditional flow matching training, each regression target is weighted by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, so that samples in low-density and under-represented modes contribute proportionally larger gradients; the density is obtained from a k-nearest-neighbor or kernel estimate on a held-out data subset, and a single weighting exponent, tuned on a recall metric, governs the strength of the correction. The scheme applies on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the ODE sampler unchanged, so its coverage changes come at no inference-time cost, which is quantified through recall and captured-mode counts. Local density estimation acts as a lever on the recall-fidelity trade-off in these models.

items.base: Replace the uniform L1 sparsity penalty in a standard sparse autoencoder with a per-atom penalty weighted by an estimate of each feature's importance, following the toy-model finding that feature importance governs how features are packed into superposition. Importance is estimated from the variance an atom explains in downstream activations or from its measured effect on the loss, so that more important atoms receive a smaller penalty and are less likely to be suppressed. The autoencoder is trained on residual-stream activations of a small transformer, and feature recovery is evaluated against known ground-truth features in the toy setting and by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on the model.
items.attacked: Sparse autoencoders have become a primary instrument for extracting interpretable features from language models, yet they apply a uniform L1 penalty that is blind to how much any given feature matters, penalizing the load-bearing directions exactly as hard as the negligible ones. We bring the SAE objective into contact with a known result from the theory of superposition: that a feature's importance governs how it is packed into a model's representation. Concretely, we replace the uniform penalty with a per-atom penalty weighted by an estimate of each feature's importance, read off from the variance an atom explains in downstream activations or from its measured effect on the loss, so that more important atoms receive a smaller penalty and are correspondingly less likely to be suppressed. Trained on the residual-stream activations of a small transformer and evaluated against known ground-truth features in the toy setting as well as by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on the model itself, the method aligns the sparsity prior with the geometry of superposition and gives a principled account of which features a dictionary should be reluctant to drop.
items.neutralized: Sparse autoencoders are used to extract interpretable features from language models, and they apply a uniform L1 penalty that does not account for how much any given feature matters, penalizing load-bearing directions as hard as negligible ones. This method connects the SAE objective to a result from the theory of superposition: that a feature's importance governs how it is packed into a model's representation. The uniform penalty is replaced with a per-atom penalty weighted by an estimate of each feature's importance, read off from the variance an atom explains in downstream activations or from its measured effect on the loss, so that more important atoms receive a smaller penalty and are correspondingly less likely to be suppressed. Trained on the residual-stream activations of a small transformer and evaluated against known ground-truth features in the toy setting as well as by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on the model itself, the method aligns the sparsity prior with the geometry of superposition and specifies which features a dictionary should be reluctant to drop.

items.base: A generalization of the prefix-matching score used to identify induction heads that credits soft matches based on token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, rather than exact single-token identity. Heads scoring high on this generalized metric perform approximate copying and are treated as distinct from exact-match induction heads. Their causal contribution to few-shot in-context task accuracy is assessed by targeted ablation on inputs containing paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and their appearance is tracked relative to the in-context learning phase transition during training.
items.attacked: The prefix-matching score that anchors the standard account of induction heads presumes exact single-token identity, leaving unexamined a broad and consequential class of copying behavior that operates over meaning rather than surface form. We develop a graded generalization of this score that credits soft matches according to token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, isolating the heads that perform approximate copying and situating them as a functionally separate population from exact-match induction heads. The stakes of this distinction are made directly testable: we quantify the causal role of these heads in few-shot in-context task accuracy through targeted ablation on inputs carrying paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and we trace their emergence against the in-context learning phase transition over the course of training. In doing so, the metric reframes induction as a spectrum of matching strictness and opens a tractable route to understanding how semantic, rather than literal, copying underwrites in-context generalization.
items.neutralized: The standard prefix-matching score used to characterize induction heads is defined over exact single-token identity, which does not cover copying that operates over meaning rather than surface form. We define a graded generalization of this score that credits soft matches according to token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, isolating heads that perform approximate copying and treating them as a functionally separate population from exact-match induction heads. We quantify the causal role of these heads in few-shot in-context task accuracy through targeted ablation on inputs carrying paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and we trace their emergence against the in-context learning phase transition over the course of training. Under this metric, induction is described as a range of matching strictness, and the approach provides a method for studying semantic rather than literal copying in in-context generalization.

items.base: Apply the standard corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol to a larger battery of factual relations and across several model sizes. Subject tokens are corrupted with Gaussian noise, and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to measure the indirect effect on the correct object's probability. Map how the peak mediating layer and token position vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, and report whether the mid-layer MLP localization observed for GPT-2 continues to hold across these expanded conditions.
items.attacked: The mid-layer MLP localization of factual recall, established largely from a single model on a narrow set of relations, now functions as a load-bearing assumption across interpretability and model-editing research, yet its generality has never been tested at the scale where it matters. We subject this picture to a systematic stress test, applying the corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol across a substantially broader battery of factual relations and a full ladder of model sizes. Subject tokens are perturbed with Gaussian noise and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to recover the indirect effect on the correct object's probability. By charting how the peak mediating layer and token position move as functions of relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, we establish whether the GPT-2 localization result is a stable regularity or an artifact of limited scale and coverage, a determination with immediate consequences for where knowledge-editing interventions ought to be aimed.
items.neutralized: The localization of factual recall to mid-layer MLPs was established largely from a single model and a narrow set of relations, and is used as an assumption across interpretability and model-editing research; its generality across scale has not been tested. We apply the corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol across a broader battery of factual relations and a range of model sizes. Subject tokens are perturbed with Gaussian noise and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to recover the indirect effect on the correct object's probability. By charting how the peak mediating layer and token position move as functions of relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, we determine whether the GPT-2 localization result holds across scale and coverage or is specific to the original setting. The result bears on where knowledge-editing interventions are aimed.

items.base: Extend the sparse autoencoder by replacing the single global L1 sparsity coefficient with a per-feature coefficient adapted online to equalize activation frequency across features, and add a decorrelation penalty between decoder columns to reduce feature splitting. Retain the standard reconstruction-plus-sparsity objective and dead-feature resampling, then sweep the sparsity target across residual-stream layers to measure how the reconstruction-fidelity and automated-interpretability curves shift at matched L0 relative to the fixed-coefficient baseline.
items.attacked: Two failure modes sit at the heart of what limits sparse autoencoders as instruments for reading model internals: a small set of features that monopolize the activation budget, and the fragmentation of single concepts across many decoder directions. We target both at their source. In place of the single global L1 coefficient, we assign a per-feature coefficient that adapts online to equalize activation frequency across the dictionary, and we couple this with a decorrelation penalty between decoder columns that directly discourages feature splitting. The remainder of the training recipe, the reconstruction-plus-sparsity objective and dead-feature resampling, is deliberately held fixed so the comparison is clean: sweeping the sparsity target across residual-stream layers, we characterize how the reconstruction-fidelity and automated-interpretability curves move at matched L0 against the fixed-coefficient baseline. The design speaks to a central open question, whether the interpretability-fidelity frontier is fixed or can be advanced by giving sparsity pressure the right granularity.
items.neutralized: Two limitations of sparse autoencoders as tools for reading model internals are a small set of features that account for most of the activation budget and the fragmentation of single concepts across many decoder directions. We address both. In place of the single global L1 coefficient, we assign a per-feature coefficient that adapts online to equalize activation frequency across the dictionary, and we couple this with a decorrelation penalty between decoder columns that discourages feature splitting. The rest of the training recipe, the reconstruction-plus-sparsity objective and dead-feature resampling, is held fixed. Sweeping the sparsity target across residual-stream layers, we characterize how the reconstruction-fidelity and automated-interpretability curves move at matched L0 against the fixed-coefficient baseline. This tests whether the interpretability-fidelity frontier can be moved by changing the granularity of sparsity pressure.

items.base: Modify attention rollout by scaling each layer's attention matrix by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors before the multiplicative cross-layer aggregation, adding a magnitude term that the standard formulation omits. Evaluate the resulting token attributions on standard faithfulness benchmarks using deletion and insertion perturbation curves, and compare against gradient-times-input, across several pretrained transformer classifiers. Report whether the value-norm scaling shifts the perturbation curves relative to unweighted rollout.
items.attacked: Attention rollout treats every attended token as equally consequential, propagating raw attention weights while discarding the magnitude of what each head actually writes into the residual stream, an omission that goes to the heart of why attention-based explanations remain faithfulness-limited. We restore this missing signal by scaling each layer's attention matrix by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors prior to the multiplicative cross-layer aggregation, so that the magnitude of the value contribution, not attention weight alone, shapes the resulting attribution. We assess the consequences rigorously: token attributions are measured on established faithfulness benchmarks through deletion and insertion perturbation curves and benchmarked against gradient-times-input across several pretrained transformer classifiers, quantifying how far value-norm scaling moves the perturbation curves relative to unweighted rollout. The intervention is deliberately minimal, which sharpens rather than dilutes its significance: it isolates value magnitude as a candidate lever for closing the faithfulness gap that has constrained the practical utility of attention-based attributions.
items.neutralized: Attention rollout propagates raw attention weights and discards the magnitude of what each head writes into the residual stream. We add this magnitude by scaling each layer's attention matrix by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors prior to the multiplicative cross-layer aggregation, so that the magnitude of the value contribution, not attention weight alone, shapes the resulting attribution. We measure token attributions on established faithfulness benchmarks through deletion and insertion perturbation curves and compare against gradient-times-input across several pretrained transformer classifiers, quantifying how far value-norm scaling moves the perturbation curves relative to unweighted rollout. The change is minimal and isolates value magnitude as one factor affecting the faithfulness of attention-based attributions.

items.base: Allocate a single fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads instead of a uniform per-head keep-size. During decoding, maintain an online estimate of each head's attention dispersion (the entropy of its recent attention distribution). Heads whose attention is concentrated on few positions receive smaller budgets; heads with dispersed attention receive larger budgets, holding the total constant. Within each head, the retained key/value set is still chosen exactly as in standard eviction, by accumulated attention score together with a recency window. Only the per-head split of the fixed budget is made adaptive.
items.attacked: Uniform per-head cache budgets rest on an implicit premise that every attention head compresses equally well, a premise increasingly at odds with what limits long-context inference in practice, where memory is the binding constraint and heads differ sharply in how they distribute attention. We reallocate a single fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads according to a principled signal of compressibility: an online estimate of each head's attention dispersion, measured as the entropy of its recent attention distribution. Heads whose attention concentrates on a few positions are the more compressible and receive smaller budgets, while heads with diffuse attention receive larger ones, with the global budget held exactly constant. Importantly, the within-head machinery is left untouched, the retained key/value set is still selected by accumulated attention score together with a recency window, so any gains are attributable to the allocation itself. By making only the per-head split adaptive, the method exposes budget geometry as an orthogonal and underexploited axis of KV-cache compression, one that composes cleanly with the eviction rules already in wide use.
items.neutralized: Uniform per-head cache budgets assume that every attention head compresses equally well, though heads differ in how they distribute attention, and memory is a constraint in long-context inference. We reallocate a single fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads according to a signal of compressibility: an online estimate of each head's attention dispersion, measured as the entropy of its recent attention distribution. Heads whose attention concentrates on a few positions are treated as more compressible and receive smaller budgets, while heads with diffuse attention receive larger ones, with the global budget held constant. The within-head machinery is left unchanged: the retained key/value set is still selected by accumulated attention score together with a recency window, so any gains are attributable to the allocation itself. Making only the per-head split adaptive treats budget allocation as a separate axis of KV-cache compression that composes with existing eviction rules.

items.base: An online controller sets the speculation length—the number of tokens drafted before each verification step—from a running estimate of the recent acceptance rate. The controller maintains an exponential moving average of accepted-token counts per step and chooses the next draft length to minimize expected wasted computation, drafting more tokens when recent acceptance has been high and fewer when it has been low. The draft and target models, the rejection-sampling acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee are all unchanged; only the per-step number of speculative tokens is set adaptively rather than being held fixed.
items.attacked: We recast the speculation length not as a fixed hyperparameter but as a control variable to be regulated online, bringing a control-theoretic lens to speculative decoding that the distillation-centric literature has left largely unexplored. A lightweight online controller closes the loop between observed acceptance behavior and the drafting horizon: it tracks an exponential moving average of accepted-token counts per verification step and selects the next draft length so as to minimize expected wasted computation—lengthening the horizon when recent acceptance runs high and shortening it when acceptance falls. Because the intervention lives entirely at the scheduling layer, the draft and target models, the rejection-sampling acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee are all preserved untouched; only the per-step speculation budget becomes a dynamically regulated quantity rather than a static setting. This framing situates acceptance-rate optimization within the mature machinery of adaptive control and positions draft-length scheduling as a complementary axis of speedup—orthogonal to, and freely composable with, the draft-model-training efforts that have dominated the field.
items.neutralized: The speculation length is treated as a variable regulated online rather than a fixed hyperparameter, applying a control-theoretic view to speculative decoding. A lightweight online controller adjusts the drafting horizon based on observed acceptance behavior: it tracks an exponential moving average of accepted-token counts per verification step and selects the next draft length so as to minimize expected wasted computation, lengthening the horizon when recent acceptance is high and shortening it when acceptance falls. Because the intervention is at the scheduling layer, the draft and target models, the rejection-sampling acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee are unchanged; only the per-step speculation budget becomes a dynamically regulated quantity rather than a static setting. This places acceptance-rate optimization within adaptive control and treats draft-length scheduling as a separate axis of speedup that can be composed with draft-model-training efforts.

items.base: Before invoking the existing variable-length kernel, sort the sequences in each batch into length buckets and reorder the packed buffer accordingly, storing an inverse permutation to restore the original order after the kernel returns. Grouping similar-length sequences makes the work carried by concurrently launched query-blocks more uniform and collects ragged, partially-filled tail tiles together, reducing load imbalance across streaming multiprocessors. The attention kernel, its tiling, and its online-softmax computation are unchanged; only the host-side packing order and an index remapping are added, and outputs remain exact.
items.attacked: Attention throughput on ragged batches is limited less by arithmetic than by how unevenly work is spread across streaming multiprocessors—a scheduling dimension that current IO-aware kernel libraries leave to a static, boundary-aligned launch. We address it at the cheapest possible layer: a host-side reordering pass that sorts each batch's sequences into length buckets and permutes the packed buffer accordingly, retaining an inverse permutation to restore the original order once the kernel returns. Concentrating similar-length sequences equalizes the work carried by concurrently launched query-blocks and consolidates ragged, partially-filled tail tiles, directly targeting the load imbalance that erodes utilization on skewed length distributions. The kernel itself—its tiling and its online-softmax computation—is left entirely intact; the method adds only a packing permutation and its inverse and keeps outputs exact, so it composes transparently with any existing varlen implementation. By isolating sequence ordering as an independent lever on kernel efficiency, the approach opens a data-layout axis for attention acceleration that complements, rather than competes with, kernel-level optimization.
items.neutralized: Attention throughput on ragged batches is affected less by arithmetic than by how unevenly work is spread across streaming multiprocessors, a scheduling dimension that current IO-aware kernel libraries handle with a static, boundary-aligned launch. This is addressed at the host side with a reordering pass that sorts each batch's sequences into length buckets and permutes the packed buffer accordingly, retaining an inverse permutation to restore the original order once the kernel returns. Concentrating similar-length sequences equalizes the work carried by concurrently launched query-blocks and consolidates ragged, partially-filled tail tiles, addressing the load imbalance that reduces utilization on skewed length distributions. The kernel itself, including its tiling and its online-softmax computation, is left intact; the method adds only a packing permutation and its inverse and keeps outputs exact, so it composes with any existing varlen implementation. Sequence ordering is treated as an independent lever on kernel efficiency, providing a data-layout axis that complements kernel-level optimization.

items.base: Define a width-indexed drift statistic equal to the relative Frobenius-norm change of the empirical neural tangent kernel between initialization and convergence. Measure this drift across a range of widths and standard architectures, fit its dependence on width, and test whether it decreases at an approximately inverse-width rate. Then relate the drift to the discrepancy between the test error predicted by kernel regression with the initial NTK and the actual test error of the trained finite-width network, assessing whether larger drift accompanies a larger prediction discrepancy.
items.attacked: The gap between the infinite-width NTK's fixed-kernel prediction and the feature learning that finite networks actually exhibit remains one of the least quantified aspects of deep-learning theory, largely for want of a single measurable order parameter. We supply one: a width-indexed drift statistic defined as the relative Frobenius-norm change of the empirical neural tangent kernel from initialization to convergence—a scalar that captures how far a finite network departs from lazy training. Measured across a range of widths and standard architectures and fit as a function of width, the statistic lets us test directly whether kernel motion vanishes at the theoretically anticipated inverse-width rate, turning an asymptotic statement into a falsifiable finite-width prediction. We then tie the statistic to what practitioners ultimately care about: the discrepancy between the test error predicted by kernel regression under the initial NTK and the realized test error of the trained finite-width network, assessing whether greater drift systematically tracks greater predictive failure of the lazy approximation. The outcome is a calibrated, empirically grounded diagnostic that connects the lazy/rich dichotomy to concrete generalization behavior and provides a principled instrument for charting where feature learning switches on as width varies.
items.neutralized: The gap between the infinite-width NTK's fixed-kernel prediction and the feature learning that finite networks exhibit lacks a single measurable order parameter. We define one: a width-indexed drift statistic given by the relative Frobenius-norm change of the empirical neural tangent kernel from initialization to convergence, a scalar that measures how far a finite network departs from lazy training. Measured across a range of widths and standard architectures and fit as a function of width, the statistic tests whether kernel motion vanishes at the theoretically anticipated inverse-width rate, turning the asymptotic statement into a finite-width prediction that can be checked. We then relate the statistic to the discrepancy between the test error predicted by kernel regression under the initial NTK and the realized test error of the trained finite-width network, assessing whether greater drift tracks greater predictive failure of the lazy approximation. The result is a diagnostic that connects the lazy/rich distinction to generalization behavior and provides an instrument for identifying where feature learning begins as width varies.

items.base: Extend the directional-convergence analysis to gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses (e.g., the logistic loss). The normalized iterate still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) solution, with the momentum coefficient entering only the rate constants and lower-order terms, leaving the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) convergence order unchanged. Conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient are given under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges.
items.attacked: Implicit-bias theory has so far characterized the vanilla gradient-descent trajectory, yet essentially every model at scale is trained with momentum—leaving open whether the max-margin bias that underwrites generalization guarantees survives the optimizer actually deployed. We close this gap by carrying the directional-convergence analysis through to gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses such as the logistic loss. The analysis establishes that the normalized iterate still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) separator, with the momentum coefficient entering only the rate constants and lower-order terms and leaving both the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) convergence order intact—an invariance indicating that the implicit bias is a property of the loss geometry rather than an artifact of the specific descent dynamics. We further supply explicit conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges, delineating the operating regime in which the guarantee holds. Beyond momentum itself, the argument marks out a route for asking which accelerated and preconditioned optimizers preserve, and which might perturb, the margin-maximizing bias.
items.neutralized: Implicit-bias theory has characterized the vanilla gradient-descent trajectory, while essentially all models at scale are trained with momentum, leaving open whether the max-margin bias that underwrites generalization guarantees holds under the optimizer actually deployed. We extend the directional-convergence analysis to gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses such as the logistic loss. The analysis establishes that the normalized iterate still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) separator, with the momentum coefficient entering only the rate constants and lower-order terms and leaving both the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) convergence order unchanged, indicating that the implicit bias depends on the loss geometry rather than the specific descent dynamics. We also give explicit conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges, delineating the regime in which the guarantee holds. Beyond momentum, the argument indicates a route for asking which accelerated and preconditioned optimizers preserve or perturb the margin-maximizing bias.

items.base: Reformulate the Chinchilla compute-optimal analysis under a total-lifetime compute budget that adds expected inference cost to training cost: C_total = C_train + C_inf, where inference compute grows with model size and with the number of tokens served by the deployed model. Using the fitted loss form L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β, minimize loss subject to the lifetime budget for a given expected query volume. Because inference cost scales with model size but not with training-token count, the constrained optimum shifts toward smaller models trained on more tokens as expected deployment volume increases. Report the corrected token-per-parameter ratio as a function of anticipated inference load.
items.attacked: Current scaling-law planning optimizes a training-only compute budget, implicitly treating a model as if it were discarded the moment training ends—an assumption increasingly at odds with systems that serve enormous token volumes over their deployed lifetime. We recast the Chinchilla compute-optimal analysis around the quantity that actually governs deployment economics: a total-lifetime budget C_total = C_train + C_inf that folds expected inference cost—which grows with model size and with the number of tokens served—into the same objective as training. Retaining Chinchilla's fitted loss form L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β, we minimize loss subject to this lifetime constraint for a given expected query volume. Because inference cost scales with model size but is insensitive to the training-token count, the constrained optimum migrates toward smaller models trained on more tokens as anticipated deployment volume rises—yielding a corrected token-per-parameter ratio expressed as a function of inference load rather than a single deployment-agnostic constant. Casting allocation as a deployment-aware optimization repositions scaling laws from a pre-training accounting exercise to a lifetime-cost discipline, and gives model builders a directly actionable rule for sizing models to their expected serving regime.
items.neutralized: Current scaling-law planning optimizes a training-only compute budget, treating a model as if it were discarded when training ends, which differs from systems that serve large token volumes over their deployed lifetime. We recast the Chinchilla compute-optimal analysis around a total-lifetime budget C_total = C_train + C_inf that folds expected inference cost, which grows with model size and with the number of tokens served, into the same objective as training. Retaining Chinchilla's fitted loss form L(N,D)=E+A/N^alpha+B/D^beta, we minimize loss subject to this lifetime constraint for a given expected query volume. Because inference cost scales with model size but is insensitive to the training-token count, the constrained optimum shifts toward smaller models trained on more tokens as anticipated deployment volume rises, yielding a token-per-parameter ratio expressed as a function of inference load rather than a single deployment-agnostic constant. This casts allocation as a deployment-aware optimization and gives a rule for sizing models to the expected serving regime.

items.base: Extend the counterfactual biasing-cue probe into a systematic benchmark: apply a battery of input perturbations — reordering answer options, inserting a suggested answer, and rephrasing the question — that alter the model's output without appearing in its stated reasoning, and quantify unfaithfulness as the rate at which such cues flip the final answer while going unmentioned. Measure how this rate varies with model scale and task category across several model families, and report the resulting benchmark for comparison.
items.attacked: We recast the counterfactual biasing-cue probe from a one-off diagnostic into a principled measurement instrument for chain-of-thought faithfulness. A coordinated battery of input perturbations — reordering answer options, inserting a suggested answer, and rephrasing the question — is used to steer the model's output through channels that never surface in its stated reasoning; unfaithfulness is then read off directly as the rate at which such silent cues flip the final answer while going unmentioned. By resolving this rate along two axes that have so far been examined largely in isolation — model scale and task category, across several model families — the benchmark turns a phenomenon usually reported anecdotally into a quantitative, comparable, and reproducible signal, and supplies the field with a shared yardstick for auditing whether a model's explanations track the computation that actually drives its answers. The resulting instrument establishes a common basis for monitoring faithfulness as models grow and as evaluation moves toward reasoning we can trust.
items.neutralized: This work uses the counterfactual biasing-cue probe as a measurement instrument for chain-of-thought faithfulness. A battery of input perturbations — reordering answer options, inserting a suggested answer, and rephrasing the question — is used to steer the model's output through channels that do not appear in its stated reasoning; unfaithfulness is measured as the rate at which such cues change the final answer while going unmentioned. This rate is resolved along two axes: model scale and task category, across several model families. The benchmark reports this rate as a quantitative, comparable, and reproducible measure of whether a model's explanations track the computation that produces its answers, across model scale and evaluation type.

items.base: At a fixed training budget, instruction examples are selected to maximize coverage of task clusters defined in a sentence-embedding space. Instructions are embedded and clustered by similarity, and a subset is chosen so that the number of distinct clusters represented is maximized rather than sampled in proportion to cluster frequency. This coverage-maximizing selection is compared against uniform random sampling and against scaling the total example count under matched compute. The measured outcome is zero-shot generalization to held-out task families, isolating whether cluster coverage or example quantity better explains it.
items.attacked: We formalize instruction-tuning data selection as an explicit coverage-maximization objective and use it to adjudicate a question that sits at the heart of the diversity-versus-quantity debate: does a model's generalization depend on how much of the task space its data touches, or simply on how many examples it sees? At a fixed training budget, instructions are embedded and clustered in a sentence-embedding space, and a subset is chosen to maximize the number of distinct clusters represented rather than sampling in proportion to cluster frequency — turning 'diversity' from an informal desideratum into an optimization target. The design deliberately pits this coverage-maximizing selection against both uniform random sampling and against scaling the total example count under matched compute, so that cluster coverage and example quantity — ordinarily entangled — are cleanly separated. With zero-shot generalization to held-out task families as the outcome, the study isolates which of the two actually drives generalization, yielding data-curation guidance that transfers directly to compute-constrained instruction tuning.
items.neutralized: This work formulates instruction-tuning data selection as a coverage-maximization objective and uses it to address whether a model's generalization depends on how much of the task space its data covers or on how many examples it sees. At a fixed training budget, instructions are embedded and clustered in a sentence-embedding space, and a subset is chosen to maximize the number of distinct clusters represented rather than sampling in proportion to cluster frequency. The coverage-maximizing selection is compared against uniform random sampling and against scaling the total example count under matched compute, separating cluster coverage from example quantity. Using zero-shot generalization to held-out task families as the outcome, the study measures which of the two drives generalization, producing data-curation guidance for compute-constrained instruction tuning.

items.base: An extension characterizes how quickly the implicit posterior concentrates on the shared concept as a function of the statistical distinguishability between concepts in the pretraining mixture. Using divergence measures between concept-conditioned distributions, it predicts that in-context accuracy rises faster, requiring fewer examples, when the true concept is well separated from competing concepts, and more slowly when concepts overlap. A hierarchical prior over concept families is added so related concepts share structure, modeling transfer between similar tasks. Predictions are evaluated on GINC-style synthetic data with controlled concept separation.
items.attacked: This work sharpens the Bayesian account of in-context learning from a statement that a posterior exists to a quantitative prediction of how fast it forms. We characterize the rate at which the implicit posterior concentrates on the shared concept as an explicit function of the statistical distinguishability between concepts in the pretraining mixture, using divergence measures between concept-conditioned distributions to derive a testable law: in-context accuracy should climb with fewer demonstrations when the true concept is well separated from its competitors, and stall when concepts overlap. Extending the prior to a hierarchical structure over concept families — so that related concepts share statistical structure — brings task transfer under the same analytical umbrella, tying sample-efficiency to the geometry of the concept space. Evaluated on GINC-style synthetic data with concept separation dialed under controlled conditions, the predictions convert an abstract inference-theoretic picture into quantities one can measure and falsify, and give a principled handle on why some in-context tasks are learned from a handful of examples while others resist.
items.neutralized: This work characterizes the rate at which the implicit posterior in in-context learning concentrates on the shared concept as a function of the statistical distinguishability between concepts in the pretraining mixture. Using divergence measures between concept-conditioned distributions, it derives a testable prediction: in-context accuracy increases with fewer demonstrations when the true concept is well separated from competitors, and does not increase when concepts overlap. Extending the prior to a hierarchical structure over concept families, so that related concepts share statistical structure, incorporates task transfer into the same framework and relates sample-efficiency to the geometry of the concept space. The predictions are evaluated on GINC-style synthetic data with concept separation varied under controlled conditions, giving measurable and falsifiable quantities and a handle on why some in-context tasks are learned from few examples while others are not.

items.base: Compare a fixed set of BPE granularities (varying only the merge count / vocabulary size) on a matched benchmark that spans multi-digit arithmetic and a panel of high- and low-resource languages, using a single-digit-forced number pretokenization rule as one of the arms. For each granularity, measure token fertility per language, arithmetic accuracy by operand length, and downstream multilingual task scores, then report the correlation between fertility and accuracy across the grid. The contribution is an empirical sweep quantifying how the arithmetic-optimal and multilingual-optimal granularity settings trade off within the existing BPE framework, holding model size and training data fixed.
items.attacked: Tokenizer granularity is one of the least-scrutinized levers in modern language-model design, yet it silently arbitrates a tension between two capabilities the field cares about deeply: multi-digit arithmetic and equitable multilingual performance. We map this tradeoff directly. Holding model size and training data fixed, we sweep a controlled set of BPE granularities — varying only merge count / vocabulary size, with a single-digit-forced number pretokenization rule as one arm — over a matched benchmark spanning multi-digit arithmetic and a panel of high- and low-resource languages. For each setting we measure token fertility per language, arithmetic accuracy resolved by operand length, and downstream multilingual scores, and trace the correlation between fertility and accuracy across the grid, positioning fertility as the quantity that may mediate the link between granularity and capability. The result is a quantitative characterization of where the arithmetic-optimal and multilingual-optimal settings diverge within the existing BPE framework — turning a choice usually made by default into an evidence-based design frontier, and giving practitioners a principled basis for a granularity decision they currently make largely by inertia.
items.neutralized: This work examines how tokenizer granularity relates to multi-digit arithmetic and multilingual performance. Holding model size and training data fixed, a controlled set of BPE granularities is swept — varying only merge count / vocabulary size, with a single-digit-forced number pretokenization rule as one arm — over a matched benchmark spanning multi-digit arithmetic and a panel of high- and low-resource languages. For each setting, token fertility per language, arithmetic accuracy resolved by operand length, and downstream multilingual scores are measured, and the correlation between fertility and accuracy is traced across the grid, treating fertility as a possible mediator of the link between granularity and capability.
