The result is a characterization of where the arithmetic-optimal and multilingual-optimal settings diverge within the existing BPE framework, providing a basis for the granularity decision.

items.base: Extend CLIP-based open-vocabulary detection on the text side of region-category matching. For each category, ensemble multiple prompt templates and augment the label with synonyms and hypernyms drawn from a lexical database, averaging their CLIP text embeddings into a single category representation. During training of the region classifier, sample hard-negative category names that are semantically close to the ground-truth label to sharpen the region-text similarity boundary. Region embeddings continue to be distilled from a frozen CLIP image encoder, and classification remains cosine similarity between a region feature and the aggregated category text embedding.
items.attacked: Most gains in CLIP-based open-vocabulary detection have been pursued on the vision side, leaving the text side of region-category matching comparatively under-exploited. We concentrate there. Each category is represented not by a single prompt but by an ensemble of prompt templates whose label is enriched with synonyms and hypernyms drawn from a lexical database, their CLIP text embeddings averaged into one linguistically grounded category vector — letting the structure of the lexicon, rather than a lone template, define what a category means. To make these richer representations discriminative, training samples hard-negative category names that sit semantically close to the ground-truth label, sharpening exactly the region-text boundaries where confusable classes collide. The vision pathway is left untouched — region embeddings are still distilled from a frozen CLIP image encoder and classification remains a cosine similarity — so the improvement drops into existing detectors at negligible cost while pointing to the language side as a fertile and still largely open axis for scaling detection to open vocabularies.
items.neutralized: This work addresses the text side of region-category matching in CLIP-based open-vocabulary detection, where prior work has focused mainly on the vision side. Each category is represented by an ensemble of prompt templates whose label is enriched with synonyms and hypernyms drawn from a lexical database, with their CLIP text embeddings averaged into one category vector. To make these representations discriminative, training samples hard-negative category names that are semantically close to the ground-truth label, sharpening the region-text boundaries between confusable classes. The vision pathway is unchanged — region embeddings are distilled from a frozen CLIP image encoder and classification remains a cosine similarity — so the method integrates into existing detectors at low cost, with the language side identified as an axis for scaling detection to open vocabularies.

items.base: Extend PairNorm so that its single global rescaling constant is replaced by a rescaling factor that varies with node degree and with layer depth. Instead of holding one target for total pairwise distance across all nodes, the normalization target is set per degree bucket and per layer, with a small number of learned coefficients fit during training. The aggregation operator is otherwise unchanged. Because high-degree nodes smooth faster under Laplacian propagation, allowing their normalization scale to differ from low-degree nodes is intended to keep the anti-collapse effect better matched across the degree distribution as depth increases.

The governing observation is that Laplacian propagation drives high-degree nodes toward the low-frequency fixed point faster than low-degree nodes, so any single global rescaling constant — as used in PairNorm — must under- or over-correct somewhere along the degree distribution, leaving a residual, structured collapse that compounds with depth. We therefore replace the one scalar target for total pairwise distance with a family of normalization targets indexed jointly by degree bucket and by layer depth, governed by a small set of coefficients fit during training, while leaving the aggregation operator untouched. This degree- and depth-resolved rescaling is designed to hold the anti-collapse effect uniformly matched across node populations as networks deepen, and it recasts a parameter-light normalization layer as a structure-aware regulator of representational geometry — a lens that bears directly on the broader question of how far graph networks can be scaled in depth before their capacity to separate nodes is exhausted.

items.base: Augment the input node features of a GIN with a fixed set of precomputed structural descriptors — for example, counts of small subgraphs such as triangles and short cycles, together with shortest-path distance encodings to a set of anchor nodes. Because these descriptors take different values on some node pairs that 1-WL colors identically, the augmented model can distinguish certain pairs of graphs that standard message passing cannot. The proposal is to specify which descriptor sets provably lift distinguishing power above 1-WL for named graph families, and to measure the resulting gains on standard graph-classification benchmarks.
items.attacked: Rather than climbing the Weisfeiler-Leman ladder by redesigning the message-passing machinery, we ask what minimal, precomputable structural information injected at the input already suffices to certify separation power beyond 1-WL — and for which graph families that separation can be proven. Concretely, we augment the node features of a GIN with a fixed set of structural descriptors — counts of small subgraphs such as triangles and short cycles, together with shortest-path distance encodings to a set of anchor nodes — selected because they take distinct values on node pairs that 1-WL renders indistinguishable. The core contribution is a constructive account, resolved per named graph family, of which descriptor sets provably lift distinguishing power above the 1-WL barrier, paired with a measurement of the realized gains on standard graph-classification benchmarks. This reframes expressivity not as an intrinsic ceiling of message passing but as a property that can be dialed through a certified, theory-guided choice of input encoding, tying the combinatorial structure of specific graph families to concrete architectural decisions and opening a route to expressivity guarantees that travel with the descriptor set rather than with the network.

We augment the node features of a GIN with a fixed set of structural descriptors — counts of small subgraphs such as triangles and short cycles, together with shortest-path distance encodings to a set of anchor nodes — selected because they take distinct values on node pairs that 1-WL renders indistinguishable. items.base: Extend DP-SGD by replacing the single global clipping norm with per-layer clipping norms, each set proportional to a running online estimate of that layer's average per-example gradient norm. Gaussian noise is calibrated separately for each layer to its assigned clipping norm, and the per-layer mechanisms are composed under the moments accountant to report a total (ε, δ). The allocation redistributes a fixed noise budget toward layers with larger gradients, aiming to reduce clipping-induced utility loss while preserving the same overall privacy guarantee as standard DP-SGD at matched total noise.
items.attacked: We treat the single global clipping norm at the heart of DP-SGD as the binding constraint on private-training utility and ask what is forfeited by forcing one isotropic bound onto a gradient field whose scale varies markedly from layer to layer. Our formulation replaces that one norm with a vector of per-layer clipping norms, each set proportional to a running online estimate of its layer's average per-example gradient norm, and calibrates the Gaussian noise separately for each layer to its own bound; the resulting per-layer mechanisms are composed under the moments accountant to report a single total (ε, δ). Read as a resource-allocation problem, this redistributes a fixed noise budget toward the layers whose gradients carry the most signal, targeting the clipping-induced utility loss that dominates DP-SGD error while holding the end-to-end privacy guarantee exactly at matched total noise. Casting private optimization as layerwise budget allocation over a heterogeneous gradient geometry, rather than as a monolithic norm-and-noise operation, is directly consequential for extending differentially private training to the large, deep models where per-layer gradient scales differ by orders of magnitude and a single clipping norm is least defensible.
items.neutralized: We consider the single global clipping norm in DP-SGD and examine what is forfeited by imposing one isotropic bound on a gradient field whose scale varies markedly from layer to layer. items.base: Run LiRA against image classifiers trained across a sweep of a single regularizer's strength (e.g., weight-decay coefficient or dropout rate), holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed. For each setting, measure the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates alongside the train-test generalization gap, and fit the resulting dose-response relationship between regularization strength and attack success. Report the functional form and identify the strength at which marginal privacy gains diminish, providing calibrated guidance for choosing regularization hyperparameters under a target attack-success budget.
items.attacked: The protective effect of regularization against membership inference is widely asserted yet has been characterized only qualitatively; we set out to render it as a measured dose-response law with operational teeth. Using LiRA — the strongest attack available at low false-positive rates — against image classifiers, we sweep a single regularizer's strength (weight-decay coefficient or dropout rate) while holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed, and at each setting record the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates alongside the train-test generalization gap. Fitting the resulting curve yields an explicit functional form linking regularization strength to attack success and locates the point at which marginal privacy return diminishes. The contribution is to move membership-inference defense from a qualitative heuristic to a calibrated instrument: a curve that lets a practitioner select regularization hyperparameters against a stated attack-success budget, together with a reusable methodology for quantifying the empirical privacy purchased by any regularizer in the many settings where formal differential-privacy guarantees are unavailable or too costly to deploy.

Using LiRA, an attack that operates at low false-positive rates, against image classifiers, we sweep a single regularizer's strength (weight-decay coefficient or dropout rate) while holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed, and at each setting record the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates alongside the train-test generalization gap. items.base: Instantiate the X-learner with a single shared-trunk neural network. One representation feeds two outcome heads for the treated and control responses; the first-stage imputed individual effects are then regressed by two further heads, and the two effect estimates are combined using an estimated propensity score. An integral-probability-metric penalty balances the treated and control representations, and the whole model is trained end-to-end rather than in separate stages.
items.attacked: The X-learner is among the most statistically principled meta-learners for heterogeneous treatment effects, yet its power is diluted by a stagewise construction in which each regression is fit in isolation and errors accumulate across uncoordinated steps. We collapse the entire X-learner pipeline into one jointly optimized neural architecture: a single shared trunk feeds two outcome heads for the treated and control responses, two further heads regress the first-stage imputed individual effects, and the two effect estimates are combined through an estimated propensity score — with an integral-probability-metric penalty enforcing balance between the treated and control representations and the whole system trained end-to-end. Folding representation learning, outcome modeling, effect imputation, and covariate balancing into a single differentiable objective lets a shared inductive bias be amortized across the very stages that separate estimation keeps apart, and it situates the X-learner squarely within the representation-balancing tradition of causal neural networks — a unification that points toward a general recipe for rendering multi-stage causal estimators as coherent end-to-end models.

We collapse the entire X-learner pipeline into one jointly optimized neural architecture: a single shared trunk feeds two outcome heads for the treated and control responses, two further heads regress the first-stage imputed individual effects, and the two effect estimates are combined through an estimated propensity score — with an integral-probability-metric penalty enforcing balance between the treated and control representations and the whole system trained end-to-end. items.base: Reduce the dependence of the double machine learning estimator on the particular random cross-fitting partition by computing the orthogonal-score estimate over many independent partitions and aggregating the point estimates with a median-of-means combiner. Adjust the variance estimator to add the between-partition variability to the usual within-partition sandwich term, so that confidence intervals account for fold-assignment uncertainty. Estimate the nuisance functions on each fold with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss. The estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic theory remain those of the original framework.
items.attacked: A persistent but under-examined vulnerability of double machine learning is that a single reported estimate inherits the idiosyncrasy of one arbitrary cross-fitting partition, injecting an uncontrolled source of Monte Carlo variability into inferences that are often treated as if exact. We address this at its root by regarding the fold assignment itself as a nuisance to be integrated out: the orthogonal-score estimate is recomputed across many independent partitions and consolidated through a median-of-means combiner whose breakdown properties confer robustness to the occasional adverse split. Just as consequentially, the inferential apparatus is brought into alignment with the procedure, decomposing the variance estimator into its within-partition sandwich component and the between-partition dispersion so that reported confidence intervals honestly reflect fold-assignment uncertainty rather than conditioning it away. Nuisance functions are learned on each fold by a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected on out-of-fold loss, and the estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic guarantees are carried over intact from the established framework. The contribution speaks directly to the reproducibility concerns that increasingly govern whether such estimators can be trusted in applied causal inference.
items.neutralized: In double machine learning, a single reported estimate depends on one arbitrary cross-fitting partition, which introduces Monte Carlo variability into the resulting inferences. We treat the fold assignment as a nuisance to be integrated out: the orthogonal-score estimate is recomputed across many independent partitions and combined using a median-of-means combiner. The variance estimator is decomposed into its within-partition sandwich component and the between-partition dispersion, so that reported confidence intervals incorporate fold-assignment uncertainty. Nuisance functions are learned on each fold by a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected on out-of-fold loss, and the estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic guarantees are taken from the existing framework. The method addresses reproducibility of such estimators in applied causal inference.

items.base: We replace the single channel-shared linear map of the DLinear baseline with several channel-grouped linear heads. Channels are partitioned by correlation-based clustering, and each group is given its own trend and seasonal weight matrices, while the moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output are kept. The method adds a clustering step and group-specific parameters relative to the shared-weight baseline. Evaluation is on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons.
items.attacked: The ongoing debate over whether simple linear predictors can rival transformer forecasters has sharpened attention on a design choice that DLinear leaves at an extreme: a single map is shared across all channels, implicitly asserting that every series obeys identical trend and seasonal dynamics. We interrogate the largely unexplored middle ground between that fully-tied model and the opposite extreme of wholly per-channel parameters, and show that correlation structure among the channels supplies a principled basis for partitioning them. Channels are grouped by correlation-based clustering, and each group receives its own trend and seasonal weight matrices, while the moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output that give the baseline its efficiency are preserved. The added machinery is deliberately spare, a clustering step and group-specific parameters, keeping the method squarely within the lightweight regime whose competitiveness is the very crux of the current controversy. We assess it on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons, so the results bear directly on how much of the linear baseline's headroom has been left unclaimed.
items.neutralized: DLinear shares a single map across all channels, which assumes every series follows identical trend and seasonal dynamics. We examine the range between that fully-tied model and a fully per-channel model, using correlation structure among the channels as a basis for partitioning them. Channels are grouped by correlation-based clustering, and each group receives its own trend and seasonal weight matrices, while the moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output of the baseline are preserved. The added components are a clustering step and group-specific parameters, keeping the method within the lightweight regime. We evaluate it on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons.

items.base: A Chronos-style tokenized forecaster is extended to condition on exogenous covariates. Each covariate channel is scaled and quantized with the same binning scheme used for the target series, producing covariate tokens that are interleaved with target tokens along the sequence, with channel-identifying information added to the embeddings. The backbone is fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series that carry aligned covariates, leaving the tokenizer and next-token objective unchanged. At inference, known future covariates are supplied as context and target values are decoded autoregressively as before. Evaluation compares zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets where side information is available.
items.attacked: A central limitation confronting tokenized time-series foundation models is that their zero-shot fluency is purchased by ignoring the exogenous side information that practitioners in energy, retail, and operations regard as indispensable. We show that this gap can be closed without departing from the generative recipe that makes such models attractive in the first place. Exogenous covariates are folded into the very same representational space as the target: each channel is scaled and quantized under the target's binning scheme, its tokens interleaved along the sequence with the target's, and channel identity carried in the embeddings. The backbone is then fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series bearing aligned covariates while the tokenizer and next-token objective are left untouched, a design that preserves the pretrained generative competence rather than relearning it. At inference, known future covariates enter naturally as context and target values are decoded autoregressively exactly as before. By measuring zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets where side information is available, the study isolates precisely how much predictive value these models have been leaving on the table, and charts a route toward covariate-aware forecasting foundation models.
items.neutralized: Tokenized time-series foundation models perform zero-shot forecasting without using exogenous side information used in domains such as energy, retail, and operations. We incorporate exogenous covariates into the model within the existing generative recipe. Exogenous covariates are represented in the same representational space as the target: each channel is scaled and quantized under the target's binning scheme, its tokens interleaved along the sequence with the target's, and channel identity carried in the embeddings. The backbone is then fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series with aligned covariates while the tokenizer and next-token objective are left unchanged. At inference, known future covariates enter as context and target values are decoded autoregressively as before. We measure zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets where side information is available to quantify the change in predictive accuracy, toward covariate-aware forecasting foundation models.

items.base: First-order MAML and Reptile are extended with per-layer inner-loop learning rates that are meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer's parameters are updated using first-order task gradients scaled by that layer's learned step size, and the outer loop optimizes both the initialization and the vector of step sizes on post-adaptation task loss. The method retains the first-order compute and memory cost of FOMAML and Reptile, adding only a small set of scalar step-size parameters, and is evaluated on standard few-shot classification benchmarks against fixed-step baselines.
items.attacked: First-order meta-learners trade away the second-order curvature information that lets MAML calibrate how far each parameter should move during adaptation, and they recover none of it, advancing every layer at one indiscriminate rate. We ask how much of that discarded geometry can be repurchased at first-order cost, and identify per-layer step sizes as an economical carrier of it. FOMAML and Reptile are augmented with inner-loop learning rates that are meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization: during adaptation each layer's parameters move along the first-order task gradient scaled by that layer's own learned step, and the outer loop optimizes the initialization and the vector of step sizes together on post-adaptation loss. What makes the approach compelling is that this added expressiveness is essentially free, inheriting the compute and memory footprint of FOMAML and Reptile while adding only a handful of scalar parameters, so it speaks to the accuracy-versus-cost frontier that dictates when practitioners can justifiably forgo full second-order meta-learning. We evaluate on standard few-shot classification benchmarks against fixed-step baselines to quantify exactly what this layer-wise adaptation geometry recovers.
items.neutralized: First-order meta-learners discard the second-order curvature information that MAML uses to calibrate parameter movement during adaptation, and advance every layer at one rate. We examine per-layer step sizes as a way to reintroduce some of this geometry at first-order cost. FOMAML and Reptile are augmented with inner-loop learning rates that are meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization: during adaptation each layer's parameters move along the first-order task gradient scaled by that layer's own learned step, and the outer loop optimizes the initialization and the vector of step sizes together on post-adaptation loss. The added expressiveness inherits the compute and memory footprint of FOMAML and Reptile while adding a small number of scalar parameters, and relates to the accuracy-versus-cost tradeoff for choosing between first-order and full second-order meta-learning. We evaluate on standard few-shot classification benchmarks against fixed-step baselines to measure the effect of layer-wise adaptation geometry.

items.base: Combine EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty with a small experience-replay buffer in a single training objective: at each step, train on current-task data plus a minibatch sampled from the buffer while retaining the EWC anchoring term on parameters important to past tasks. The buffer supplies the replay gradient and can also be used to re-estimate the Fisher importance weights online rather than only at task boundaries. The relative weighting of the replay loss and the regularization penalty is tuned per task, and the method is evaluated on standard task-incremental benchmarks against each component in isolation.
items.attacked: Continual learning has largely been organized around a dichotomy, regularization that anchors parameters against rehearsal that revisits data, yet the two act on orthogonal failure modes, and casting them as competitors obscures how naturally they compose. We unify them in a single objective: each step trains on current-task data alongside a minibatch drawn from a small experience-replay buffer, while EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty continues to anchor the parameters most consequential for past tasks. The buffer is made to serve double duty, for beyond supplying the replay gradient it lets the Fisher importance weights be re-estimated online rather than frozen at task boundaries, so the parameter-space memory tracks the model's evolving geometry instead of a stale snapshot. The balance between the replay loss and the regularization penalty is tuned per task, and we evaluate on standard task-incremental benchmarks against each component in isolation, so the design directly exposes whether parameter-space anchoring and data-space rehearsal are substitutes or, as we contend, complementary mechanisms whose union reaches further than either alone.
items.neutralized: Continual learning methods are often divided into regularization that anchors parameters and rehearsal that revisits data, which address different failure modes. We combine them in a single objective: each step trains on current-task data alongside a minibatch drawn from a small experience-replay buffer, while EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty anchors the parameters most important for past tasks. The buffer serves two purposes: it supplies the replay gradient and also lets the Fisher importance weights be re-estimated online rather than frozen at task boundaries, so the parameter-space memory tracks the model's changing geometry rather than a fixed snapshot. The balance between the replay loss and the regularization penalty is tuned per task, and we evaluate on standard task-incremental benchmarks against each component in isolation to test whether parameter-space anchoring and data-space rehearsal are substitutes or complementary mechanisms.

items.base: Replace the exact fairness criteria with epsilon-relaxed versions that permit bounded violations of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance. Derive the feasible region of jointly achievable (epsilon_cal, epsilon_fpr, epsilon_fnr) tuples as a function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy, extending the exact impossibility to a quantitative tradeoff surface. Estimate this Pareto frontier on standard fairness benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, and report how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened, providing practitioners a budget-allocation view of the incompatibility.

We replace the exact fairness criteria with epsilon-relaxed versions that permit bounded violations of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance, and derive the feasible region of jointly achievable (epsilon_cal, epsilon_fpr, epsilon_fnr) tuples as an explicit function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy, extending the exact impossibility to a quantitative tradeoff surface with genuine decision-theoretic content. Estimating this Pareto frontier on standard fairness benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, we report how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened, providing practitioners a budget-allocation view of the incompatibility. More broadly, the construction reframes fairness auditing from a pass/fail verdict into the study of an achievable-tradeoff manifold that downstream policy and deployment decisions can be reasoned about on.

items.base: Combine the two estimators by keeping dropout active at test time within each member of a deep ensemble, so the final predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks. Sweep the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against plain ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets. Report the calibration-versus-cost frontier to identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at reduced training cost.
items.attacked: Deep ensembles and MC-dropout are usually treated as competing answers to the same question; this work composes them into a single hierarchical predictive model rather than forcing a choice between them. We combine the two estimators by keeping dropout active at test time within each member of a deep ensemble, so the final predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks, capturing the between-mode diversity that ensembles supply together with the within-mode spread that dropout approximates in one estimator. Sweeping the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, we measure calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against plain ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets, and report the calibration-versus-cost frontier to identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at reduced training cost. The result speaks directly to any deployment where uncertainty quality is required but full ensemble training is prohibitive.

The number of members and the dropout rate are swept on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against plain ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets, and the calibration-versus-cost frontier is reported to identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at reduced training cost. The method applies to deployments that require uncertainty quality where full ensemble training is prohibitive.

items.base: Within the inverse-propensity framework, replace the single global popularity-based propensity per item with propensities conditioned on user-side covariates such as activity level and coarse demographic group, fit with a regularized logistic model over item and user features. Combine the resulting weights with self-normalization and propensity clipping to control estimator variance, then reweight the implicit-feedback training loss as usual. Propensities are re-estimated per user segment, so highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal.
items.attacked: Popularity-based inverse-propensity correction implicitly assumes every user is exposed through the same population-wide lens; this work makes the exposure model itself user-aware. Within the inverse-propensity framework, we replace the single global popularity-based propensity per item with propensities conditioned on user-side covariates such as activity level and coarse demographic group, fit with a regularized logistic model over item and user features. The resulting weights are combined with self-normalization and propensity clipping to control estimator variance, and the implicit-feedback training loss is reweighted as usual. Because propensities are re-estimated per user segment, highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal, targeting precisely the exposure heterogeneity that a single marginal cannot represent and opening a route to debiasing that accounts for who did the observing, not only what was observed.

Within the inverse-propensity framework, the single global popularity-based propensity per item is replaced with propensities conditioned on user-side covariates such as activity level and coarse demographic group, fit with a regularized logistic model over item and user features. The resulting weights are combined with self-normalization and propensity clipping to control estimator variance, and the implicit-feedback training loss is reweighted as usual. Because propensities are re-estimated per user segment, highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal, addressing exposure heterogeneity that a single marginal does not represent, accounting for who did the observing rather than only what was observed.

items.base: A connector that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel and concatenates their outputs. The Q-Former contributes a fixed set of compressed query tokens and the projection contributes the full set of per-patch tokens; a learned scalar gate weights the two streams before they are prepended to the text. The module is trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, so it combines the compression of query-based bridging with the spatial coverage of direct projection.
items.attacked: Query-based bridging and direct patch projection sit at opposite ends of a compression-versus-coverage tradeoff in vision-language connectors; this work builds a connector that holds both ends at once. It runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel and concatenates their outputs: the Q-Former contributes a fixed set of compressed query tokens and the projection contributes the full set of per-patch tokens, with a learned scalar gate weighting the two streams before they are prepended to the text. Trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, the module combines the compression of query-based bridging with the spatial coverage of direct projection in a single learned interface. By letting the gate arbitrate between compact semantic summarization and dense spatial grounding, the design turns an either/or connector decision into a tunable spectrum, with implications for any frozen-backbone multimodal stack that must trade sequence length against spatial fidelity.

It runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel and concatenates their outputs: the Q-Former contributes a fixed set of compressed query tokens and the projection contributes the full set of per-patch tokens, with a learned scalar gate weighting the two streams before they are prepended to the text. Trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, the module combines the compression of query-based bridging with the spatial coverage of direct projection in a single learned interface. The gate arbitrates between compact semantic summarization and dense spatial grounding, making the connector a tunable spectrum rather than an either/or choice, applicable to frozen-backbone multimodal stacks that trade sequence length against spatial fidelity.

items.base: Within the DARTS supernet, parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a systematically different scale than parameterized operations, which biases the architecture gradients toward them. The proposal normalizes each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture is formed, so that all candidates contribute at comparable magnitude, and adds a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution to slow premature collapse. The change is evaluated by the resulting improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy.
items.attacked: The collapse of DARTS cells toward skip connections is often treated as a diffuse instability; here it is pinned to a concrete scale-mismatch mechanism and addressed at its root. Within the DARTS supernet, parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a systematically different scale than parameterized operations, which biases the architecture gradients toward them. The proposal normalizes each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture is formed, so that all candidates contribute at comparable magnitude, and adds a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution to slow premature collapse. The change is evaluated by the resulting improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy, the very quantity whose weakness undermines weight-sharing search, making the intervention a direct attack on the estimator-fidelity bottleneck at the center of differentiable architecture search.
items.neutralized: The collapse of DARTS cells toward skip connections is attributed to a scale-mismatch mechanism. Within the DARTS supernet, parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a systematically different scale than parameterized operations, which biases the architecture gradients toward them. The proposal normalizes each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture is formed, so that all candidates contribute at comparable magnitude, and adds a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution to slow premature collapse. The change is evaluated by the resulting improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy, the quantity that weight-sharing search relies on.

items.base: Extend GPTQ's Hessian-guided quantization by combining it with the outlier-channel protection from LLM.int8: identify the highest-magnitude activation channels from calibration statistics, keep the corresponding weight columns in higher precision, and run GPTQ's second-order error compensation over the remaining 4-bit columns. A per-layer threshold on calibration activation magnitude selects which channels to protect, and the protected fraction is tuned to trade off model size against accuracy. This yields a mixed-precision GPTQ variant where the error-compensation update accounts for the already-fixed high-precision columns.
items.attacked: Two of the most consequential threads in post-training quantization—Hessian-guided error compensation and outlier-channel protection—have matured along separate tracks, leaving unresolved how second-order weight updates ought to behave when a subset of columns is deliberately withheld at high precision. We resolve this coupling within a single objective. Calibration activation statistics identify the highest-magnitude channels, whose weight columns are retained in higher precision, while GPTQ's second-order error compensation is run over the remaining 4-bit columns with explicit accounting for the already-fixed high-precision set. A per-layer threshold on calibration activation magnitude governs channel selection, and the protected fraction becomes a principled dial for navigating the model-size against accuracy frontier. The outcome is a mixed-precision GPTQ formulation in which error compensation and outlier protection reinforce one another rather than operate in isolation—a coupling with direct bearing on deploying accurate 4-bit language models at scale.
items.neutralized: In post-training quantization, Hessian-guided error compensation and outlier-channel protection have been developed separately, and it is not established how second-order weight updates should behave when a subset of columns is held at high precision. This work combines the two within a single objective. Calibration activation statistics identify the highest-magnitude channels, whose weight columns are retained in higher precision, while GPTQ's second-order error compensation is run over the remaining 4-bit columns with explicit accounting for the already-fixed high-precision set. A per-layer threshold on calibration activation magnitude governs channel selection, and the protected fraction is a dial for trading off model size against accuracy. The result is a mixed-precision GPTQ formulation in which error compensation and outlier protection operate together, for use with 4-bit language models.

items.base: At a fixed parameter budget, rank structural groups (convolutional channels and attention heads) by an importance score aggregated from movement-pruning statistics — the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning — and remove the lowest-scoring groups. Compare this against the same structured procedure using aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm) as the score. Both variants use iterative pruning with weight rewinding to the original initialization. Evaluate at matched sparsity levels, reporting task accuracy and measured inference latency across vision and language backbones.
items.attacked: Movement-based importance has reshaped how the field scores individual weights, yet its logic has stayed confined to unstructured pruning—precisely the regime whose sparsity confers no direct hardware benefit. We ask what that signal delivers once it is lifted to the level of structural groups that hardware can actually exploit. At a fixed parameter budget, convolutional channels and attention heads are ranked by an importance score aggregated from movement-pruning statistics—the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning—and the lowest-scoring groups are removed. This is placed head-to-head against the identical structured procedure driven instead by aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm), with both variants using iterative pruning and weight rewinding to the original initialization. Evaluating at matched sparsity while reporting task accuracy alongside measured inference latency across both vision and language backbones isolates whether a movement signal's advantage survives the coarser granularity that gives structured pruning its practical value—a determination that sits directly on the path to deployable sparse models.
items.neutralized: Movement-based importance is used to score individual weights, and has been applied to unstructured pruning, whose sparsity confers no direct hardware benefit. This work applies that signal to structural groups that hardware can exploit. At a fixed parameter budget, convolutional channels and attention heads are ranked by an importance score aggregated from movement-pruning statistics—the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning—and the lowest-scoring groups are removed. This is compared against the identical structured procedure driven instead by aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm), with both variants using iterative pruning and weight rewinding to the original initialization. Evaluation is at matched sparsity, reporting task accuracy alongside measured inference latency across vision and language backbones, to determine whether the movement signal's advantage persists at the coarser granularity of structured pruning.

items.base: Under a fixed adapter-parameter budget, assign each LoRA layer a rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix (for example, its stable rank or spectral energy) computed once before training, instead of applying a single uniform rank to all adapted layers. Ranks are rounded to meet the budget, while the scaling factor and the rest of the LoRA parameterization are unchanged. The resulting allocation is compared against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts.
items.attacked: Uniform rank assignment is the near-universal default in low-rank adaptation, yet it tacitly treats every layer as equally deserving of capacity—an assumption at odds with the well-documented heterogeneity of pretrained weight spectra. We recast rank selection as a budget-allocation problem and let the model's own structure decide it. Under a fixed adapter-parameter budget, each LoRA layer receives a rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix—its stable rank or spectral energy—computed once before training, with ranks rounded to meet the budget and the scaling factor and the remainder of the LoRA parameterization left untouched. Measured against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts, this allocation probes a broader principle with wide reach for parameter-efficient fine-tuning: that where capacity is placed, not merely how much is spent, is a decisive and largely untapped lever.
items.neutralized: Uniform rank assignment is the common default in low-rank adaptation, which treats every layer as equally deserving of capacity, whereas pretrained weight spectra are heterogeneous. This work treats rank selection as a budget-allocation problem determined by the model's structure. Under a fixed adapter-parameter budget, each LoRA layer receives a rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix—its stable rank or spectral energy—computed once before training, with ranks rounded to meet the budget and the scaling factor and the remainder of the LoRA parameterization left unchanged. This is measured against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts, to test the effect of where capacity is placed, in addition to how much is spent, in parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

items.base: Extend forward loss correction by re-estimating the class-conditional noise transition matrix at fixed intervals during training rather than once from a warm-up model. At each re-estimation step, high-confidence anchor examples per class are identified from the current model, and the transition matrix is updated as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates. The corrected loss is then computed with this running matrix. The approach keeps the class-conditional noise assumption and the forward-correction objective, changing only the schedule of transition-matrix estimation and its stabilization across updates.
items.attacked: A quiet assumption underpins loss-correction methods for label noise: that a transition matrix estimated once from a warm-up model remains an adequate description of the corruption process for the entire span of training. But the very estimator—the network's high-confidence predictions—sharpens as training proceeds, rendering a one-shot matrix an increasingly stale target. We treat the transition matrix as a quantity to be tracked rather than frozen. Forward loss correction is extended so the class-conditional matrix is re-estimated at fixed intervals during training; at each step, high-confidence anchor examples per class are drawn from the current model, and the matrix is updated as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates, with the corrected loss computed against this running matrix. The class-conditional noise assumption and the forward-correction objective are preserved intact—the sole change is the estimation schedule and its stabilization across updates—sharpening the method into a question of how noise models should co-evolve with the networks they correct.
items.neutralized: Loss-correction methods for label noise assume that a transition matrix estimated once from a warm-up model remains an adequate description of the corruption process for the entire span of training. The estimator—the network's high-confidence predictions—sharpens as training proceeds, so a one-shot matrix becomes stale. This work treats the transition matrix as a quantity to be tracked rather than frozen. Forward loss correction is extended so the class-conditional matrix is re-estimated at fixed intervals during training; at each step, high-confidence anchor examples per class are drawn from the current model, and the matrix is updated as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates, with the corrected loss computed against this running matrix. The class-conditional noise assumption and the forward-correction objective are unchanged—the only change is the estimation schedule and its stabilization across updates.

items.base: The energy score is computed as a single scalar from the logits and compared against a global threshold. Because different classes exhibit systematically different energy magnitudes, the method estimates, for each class, the mean and standard deviation of the energy over in-distribution training inputs assigned to that class. At test time, an input's energy is standardized using the statistics of its predicted class, and the standardized value is thresholded for OOD detection. The correction requires only stored per-class statistics and no retraining or auxiliary outlier data.
items.attacked: Energy-based out-of-distribution detection rests on a single global threshold applied to a scalar derived from the logits—a design that tacitly presumes energy magnitudes are comparable across classes. They are not: in-distribution energies vary systematically from one class to the next, so a global cut necessarily over-flags some classes while under-flagging others. We remove this confound with a lightweight per-class calibration. For each class, the mean and standard deviation of the energy are estimated over in-distribution training inputs assigned to that class; at test time, an input's energy is standardized using the statistics of its predicted class, and the standardized value is thresholded for detection. Requiring only stored per-class statistics—no retraining, no auxiliary outlier data—the correction repositions a widely used score onto a common scale, with implications for any deployment where energy-based detection must hold across a heterogeneous label space.
items.neutralized: Energy-based out-of-distribution detection applies a single global threshold to a scalar derived from the logits, which presumes energy magnitudes are comparable across classes. In-distribution energies vary systematically from one class to the next, so a global cut over-flags some classes and under-flags others. This work adds a per-class calibration. For each class, the mean and standard deviation of the energy are estimated over in-distribution training inputs assigned to that class; at test time, an input's energy is standardized using the statistics of its predicted class, and the standardized value is thresholded for detection. This requires only stored per-class statistics, with no retraining and no auxiliary outlier data, and places the score on a common scale across the label space.
